macro_segments.title: Two Wives of One Husband Without Prior Children: Confusion of Identity and Payment Obligations

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Mishnah: Two wives without prior children bearing two males
Death within thirty days: Return of payment and identification issues
Payment to one Kohen versus two Kohanim: Recovery principles
Cases of male and female births: Uncertainty of obligated party
One wife with prior children and one without: Certainty of obligation

macro_segments.title: Two Wives of Two Different Husbands Without Prior Children: Shared Obligation and Confusion

micro_segments.title: Introduction: Two separate fathers, two male children, confused identity
Payment to one Kohen and recovery issues without power of attorney
Power of attorney as solution: Transferring rights and admissions of debt
Payment to two separate Kohanim and inability to recover
Male and female births with confused paternity
Limits of power of attorney on chattels versus real estate
Rav Huna's expansion of cases where Kohen receives nothing

macro_segments.title: Death of the Firstborn: Timing and Obligation Before and After Thirty Days

micro_segments.title: Return of payment if child dies before thirty days
Obligation to pay continues if child dies after thirty days
Blessing and sad scenario of redeeming deceased child after day 30

macro_segments.title: The Ambiguous Status of Day Thirty: Debate Between Rav Gidel and Rabbi Akiva

micro_segments.title: Rav Gidel's position: Day thirty belongs to the first month, effect begins day 31
Rabbi Akiva's uncertainty: Ambiguous status of day thirty allows retention of funds
Gemara's derivation from 'month and above' in Bamidbar census
Two verses and the principle that redundancy indicates exceptions

macro_segments.title: Viability and Mourning Laws: Consensus on Leniency for Ambiguous Day Thirty

micro_segments.title: Rava's statement: All agree day thirty follows previous day for mourning purposes
Discussion of full-term versus questionable births and thirty-day viability window
Distinction between viability requirements and Pidyon HaBen timing

macro_segments.title: Father's Death and Child's Redemption Obligations: Presumptions and Proof

micro_segments.title: Father dies before day thirty: Presumption of non-redemption unless proven otherwise
Father dies after day thirty: Presumption of redemption unless proven otherwise
Nature of proof required: Witnesses or deathbed statement

macro_segments.title: Son's Own Redemption and Dual Obligations: Primary Mitzva of Self versus Child

micro_segments.title: Case where son must redeem himself and has firstborn son
Sages' position: Self redemption comes first
Rabbi Yehuda's position: Son's mitzva is father's obligation; child comes first
Practical concerns about remembering and Beit Din appointment for minors
Artificial insemination cases and community obligation

macro_segments.title: Redeeming Within Thirty Days: Debate Between Rav and Shmuel on Timing of Effect

micro_segments.title: Rav's position: Redemption within thirty days works if intent is postponed effect
Shmuel's position: Redemption must occur after thirty days; act cannot be separated from effect
Distinction between current effect and money spent: Money must exist on day 31
Comparison to Kiddushin: Ability to perform now determines ability to separate act and effect
Gemara's ruling: Shmuel's position accepted despite general rule favoring Rav in issurim
Mishnah's cases understood as requiring money to exist on day 31

macro_segments.title: Self-Redemption Versus Son-Redemption: Fundamental Nature of the Mitzva and Priority

micro_segments.title: Tannaitic dispute: Self comes first versus son comes first
Rabbi Yirmiyah's reframing: Priority determined by scarcity of funds
Consensus when funds are limited: Self comes first due to personal mitzva
Rabbi Yehuda's lien theory: Lien on ancestral field allows son's mitzva priority
Sages' rejection of lien: No automatic lien; personal status takes priority
Conceptual question: Whether mitzva is fundamentally on father or child
